stories.headline: Denver City Council approves $2.87 million in settlements tied to 2020 George Floyd protest policing
stories.summary:
The Denver City Council on Monday approved approximately $2.87 million in settlements to be paid to 13 individuals who alleged city police violated their constitutional rights during the 2020 George Floyd protests. The plaintiffs, whose individual payouts range from $140,000 to $300,000, claimed officers unlawfully used rubber bullets, pepper balls, and tear gas against demonstrators, violating their First, Fourth, and Fourteenth Amendment rights.

Councilwoman Shontel Lewis noted that funding the settlement required a $3 million rescission from the city's general fund contingency rather than from agency-specific budgets. The case was filed in U.S. District Court in 2022, four years after large-scale protests erupted in Denver following the murder of George Floyd by a Minneapolis police officer.

With Monday's approval, Denver's total payouts related to the George Floyd protests have reached $21.4 million, according to the City Attorney's Office. That figure does not include an additional $14 million a federal judge ordered the city to pay in April to a separate group of protesters, nor the $2.87 million approved Monday.

stories.headline: Two Jefferson County Democrats compete to replace term-limited Secretary of State Jena Griswold
stories.summary:
The Democratic primary for Colorado secretary of state features state Sen. Jessie Danielson of Wheat Ridge and Jefferson County Clerk and Recorder Amanda Gonzalez, both of whom have built careers around expanding voting rights. The two are vying to succeed term-limited Secretary of State Jena Griswold, with the race drawing heightened attention amid President Trump's ongoing challenges to mail-in voting systems.

Danielson, 48, helped write Colorado's mail-in ballot laws while serving as state director for America Votes and has sponsored multiple voter-rights bills during two terms in the state Senate. Gonzalez, 41, brings direct experience administering elections as Jefferson County's top elections official. Both candidates have rejected claims about a stolen 2020 election and pledged to defend Colorado's vote-by-mail system.

The Republican primary is uncontested, and the Democratic and Libertarian primary winners will face James Wiley, a self-described 'Dark MAGA' candidate, in November. The Libertarian primary pits Alex Astley of Holyoke against Sean Vadney of Highlands Ranch.

stories.headline: Colorado signs coal pollution control law in response to federal orders keeping Craig Unit 1 online
stories.summary:
Gov. Jared Polis signed House Bill 26-1226 into law, requiring installation of modern pollution controls and cost reporting for any Colorado coal-fired power plants that continue operating beyond their planned retirement dates. The legislation was drafted in direct response to U.S. Department of Energy orders that extended the operation of Craig Unit 1 in Moffat County past its planned 2025 retirement, citing electricity shortfalls in the Western region.

The law requires the Air Quality Control Commission to adopt emission limits and mandates advanced pollution control systems — including selective catalytic reduction for nitrogen oxides — on qualifying coal units still operating from 2037 onward. It also directs the Public Utilities Commission to support resource planning consistent with state clean energy goals.

Supporters, including bill sponsor Rep. Meg Froelich of Arvada, called the measure a necessary guardrail against federal overreach. Critics, including energy analyst Amy Oliver Cooke, argued the bill continues a pattern of penalizing reliable generation before adequate replacements are in place, noting residential electricity rates have risen 35% during Polis' tenure.

stories.headline: University of Denver announces major academic restructuring, closing five departments amid budget pressures
stories.summary:
The University of Denver announced Tuesday a sweeping academic restructuring that will close five departments and merge schools across the 11,500-student private university. The eliminated departments include Religious Studies, Electrical and Computer Engineering, Philosophy, Gender Women's and Sexuality Studies, and Socio-Legal Studies, though university officials said all associated academic programs will continue under restructured arrangements.

Provost Elizabeth Loboa and Chancellor Jeremy Haefner said the restructuring, developed over years of committee work with faculty and staff, is intended to make DU more agile in matching student demand — and is distinct from efforts to close a previously announced $20 million to $30 million budget shortfall. About 9% of DU's faculty accepted buyouts, and the university said it will operate under a balanced budget beginning July 1.

DU's moves reflect broader pressures facing universities nationwide, including falling enrollment, Trump administration funding cuts, and growing scrutiny of higher education's value. The school cited reductions in international student enrollment and overall campus population as key drivers of its financial challenges.

stories.headline: Wyoming deputy shot near Colorado border; suspect killed after multistate law enforcement pursuit
stories.summary:
A Carbon County, Wyoming sheriff's deputy was shot multiple times Monday after responding to a report of a man with a gun in Baggs, Wyoming, approximately three miles north of the Colorado border. The suspect opened fire on the deputy, struck him several times, and fled south in a truck before law enforcement from both Wyoming and Colorado gave chase.

Colorado's Northwest Regional SWAT Team — drawing agencies from Moffat County, Routt County, Craig, Steamboat Springs, and Hayden — responded as the suspect crossed into Colorado's jurisdictional reach. The man fired again at officers before driving off the highway and coming to a stop; he was found dead at the scene, though investigators have not yet determined whether he died from gunfire or the crash.

The injured deputy was transported to St. Mary's Medical Center in Grand Junction. The Wyoming Division of Criminal Investigation is leading the ongoing investigation, and the suspect has not been publicly identified.

stories.headline: Cherry Creek School District under federal investigation for alleged racially discriminatory programming
stories.summary:
The U.S. Department of Education has opened an investigation into Cherry Creek School District following multiple complaints alleging racially discriminatory programming within the district. Cherry Creek Schools serves more than 53,000 students across eight municipalities in the Denver metro area, spanning 108 square miles.

Details of the specific complaints and the programming under scrutiny were not fully disclosed in initial reports, but the investigation adds Cherry Creek to a list of school districts facing federal civil rights scrutiny. The district has described itself as a diverse K-12 system where inclusive excellence and college and career preparedness are priorities.

The investigation comes amid a broader national push by the Trump administration to scrutinize diversity-related programs in public schools and universities. It is not yet known how long the federal review process will take or what remedies could be required.

stories.headline: Denver tornado sirens activated by mistake during Monday severe weather; city cites human error
stories.summary:
Denver's Outdoor Warning Siren System was accidentally triggered Monday afternoon during a period of severe thunderstorms across the metro area, prompting concern among residents who mistook the alert for a confirmed tornado warning. The city's 86-siren network — 75 in Denver and 11 at Denver International Airport — is typically activated only for confirmed tornadoes or other imminent emergencies.

City officials initially investigated whether the false activation was due to computer or human error. By Tuesday, the Denver Office of Emergency Management confirmed that 'human error' and 'an improper understanding of protocol' were responsible for the inadvertent alert.

Residents expressed concern that repeated false alarms could erode public trust in the siren system, a worry echoed by neighbors in Capitol Hill and City Park. Monday's storms did produce severe weather conditions across the metro and eastern plains, with a tornado watch in effect until 9 p.m. for areas east of Interstate 25.

stories.headline: I-70 closed in both directions near Glenwood Springs after Paradise Fire breaks out
stories.summary:
Interstate 70 was shut down in both directions near Glenwood Springs on Tuesday after a brush fire ignited along the westbound lanes at milepost 112 in South Canyon. The blaze, dubbed the Paradise Fire, was initially estimated at 20 acres before being revised to 8 to 10 acres as crews gained better visibility, according to Glenwood Springs Fire Department Public Information Officer Mina Bolton.

The Glenwood Springs Fire Department responded to the fire and shared real-time updates via social media as crews worked to contain the flames. The closure of I-70 — a critical corridor connecting Denver to western Colorado and beyond — created significant traffic disruptions for travelers along the Front Range and Western Slope.

The incident comes during a period of elevated fire danger across Colorado, with Red Flag Warnings in effect Tuesday for the Front Range, Denver metro, Eastern Plains, and much of the Western Slope due to dry conditions and strong winds.

stories.headline: Avalanche captain Gabriel Landeskog wins 2026 Mark Messier NHL Leadership Award
stories.summary:
Colorado Avalanche captain Gabriel Landeskog was named Tuesday as the winner of the 2026 Mark Messier NHL Leadership Award, given annually to the player who best exemplifies leadership on and off the ice. Landeskog, who has served as Colorado's captain since 2012 and led the team to the 2022 Stanley Cup, accepted the honor with characteristic humility, crediting his teammates for making the recognition possible.

The award comes after a remarkable comeback season for the Swedish forward, who missed three full regular seasons following four knee surgeries and became the first NHL player to return after knee cartilage replacement surgery. He appeared in the first game of the 2025 Stanley Cup Playoffs and followed that with a 60-game regular season this year, posting 35 points, and 11 points in 13 playoff games.

Landeskog also captained Sweden at the 2026 Winter Olympics, further cementing his status as one of the game's premier leaders. In 798 regular-season games with Colorado, Landeskog has accumulated 606 career points.

stories.headline: Top Rockies prospect Cole Carrigg called up for MLB debut against Cubs at Coors Field
stories.summary:
The Colorado Rockies promoted outfielder Cole Carrigg from Triple-A Albuquerque on Tuesday, with the 24-year-old set to make his Major League Baseball debut against the Chicago Cubs at Coors Field. Carrigg, ranked as Colorado's sixth-best prospect by MLB Pipeline, will start in center field and bat seventh in the 5:40 p.m. first pitch.

Carrigg has had an outstanding 2026 season at Albuquerque, slashing .338/.414/.529 with six home runs, 27 RBI, and 30 stolen bases, earning Isotopes Player of the Month honors for May after batting .351 with a 1.031 OPS. The Rockies selected the Turlock, California native with the 65th overall pick in the 2023 MLB Draft following three seasons at San Diego State University.

Carrigg's debut marks a rare bright spot for the rebuilding Rockies franchise and a significant milestone for the young speedster, who can play both outfield and shortstop. He will become the ninth alumnus of Turlock High School to play in Major League Baseball.

stories.headline: Ed Sheeran to perform at Empower Field on July 4 as part of new 'LOOP' tour
stories.summary:
British musician Ed Sheeran will return to Denver on July 4 for a concert at Empower Field at Mile High as part of his new 'LOOP' tour, event organizers announced. The show will feature an all-new set design along with songs from his recently released album 'Play,' alongside classic fan favorites, with Myles Smith and Ellie Banke serving as opening acts.

Sheeran's last Denver appearance, in August 2023 on his 'Mathematics' tour, set an Empower Field attendance record with 85,233 fans and included a three-hour lightning delay. Tickets for the July 4 show are available through EdSheeran.com and Ticketmaster starting around $93.

The Independence Day concert date is expected to draw a large crowd to the stadium, adding a major entertainment event to the metro area's holiday weekend.

stories.headline: Chef Troy Guard plans five new Denver-area restaurant openings over two years, including DTC surf club concept
stories.summary:
Tag Restaurant Group founder Chef Troy Guard announced plans to open five new restaurants over the next two years, with one of the first to be G&G Surf Club in the Denver Tech Center. The expansion signals continued confidence in Denver's dining market from one of its most prominent restaurateurs, whose Guard and Grace steakhouse helped revitalize a once-underperforming area of downtown Denver.

Separately, Shea Properties received zoning approval to develop four additional acres adjacent to its Marina Square project in the Denver Tech Center, land owned by U.S. Bank. The back-to-back developments underscore the DTC's continued emergence as a hub for commercial and culinary investment in the metro's south suburbs.

The announcements come as Denver's restaurant and real estate sectors navigate ongoing pressures including rising costs and shifting consumer demand, with established operators like Guard leveraging brand recognition to fuel growth.

weather.notable: Nearly 6 degrees above the average high for this time of year.

joke.joke: Sunscreen is basically just paying money to not have fun at the beach.

topStory.headline: U.S. Strikes Iranian Defense Sites After Apache Helicopter Downed Over Strait of Hormuz; Iran Retaliates Against American Bases
topStory.summary:
The United States launched retaliatory airstrikes against Iran on Tuesday after a U.S. Army AH-64 Apache helicopter was shot down while patrolling the Strait of Hormuz, sparking a rapid exchange of military attacks between the two countries. U.S. Central Command said strikes began at 5 p.m. ET at President Donald Trump's direction and concluded shortly after 9 p.m. ET, targeting Iranian air defense systems, ground control stations, and surveillance radar sites at roughly 20 locations along the Iranian coastline, including sites on Qeshm Island and near Bandar Abbas, Goruk, and Jask.

The two American pilots aboard the downed helicopter were rescued within approximately two hours by an unmanned surface vessel — a drone boat operated by Task Force 59 — in what U.S. officials described as the first such rescue of its kind by American forces. Both crew members were recovered off the coast of Oman and were reported to be in stable condition, though they required medical attention. A U.S. official indicated the helicopter was most likely brought down by an Iranian drone.

Iran denied direct responsibility for the incident but launched a large-scale retaliatory response after the U.S. strikes, firing 21 missiles and drones at American military targets in Bahrain, Jordan, and Kuwait, according to U.S. officials. Most of the incoming projectiles were intercepted. Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi warned that foreign forces near Iranian territory face constant risk but stopped short of acknowledging any Iranian role in downing the helicopter, stating that the best solution was for U.S. forces to leave the region.

The exchange of strikes injected fresh turbulence into ongoing negotiations between Washington and Tehran, which Trump had described as recently as Monday as being in their "final throes." Following the overnight escalation, Trump posted on Truth Social that Iran had taken "too long" to agree to a deal and would "have to pay the price," declaring that "The Bully of the Middle East is DEAD." Global markets reacted sharply, with S&P 500 futures falling roughly 1 percent, Nasdaq 100 futures dropping 1.3 percent, and Dow futures declining 460 points in early trading.

Analysts cautioned against interpreting the exchange as a full return to open warfare. Firas Maksad of the Eurasia Group told the BBC that the U.S. strikes were "carefully choreographed" and limited in scope, targeting radar and coastal military facilities rather than broader Iranian infrastructure. Maksad characterized the situation as a "managed conflict" in which neither side seeks to fully resume hostilities, while noting that Iran continues to test American patience in negotiations and that the longer-term trajectory of any diplomatic agreement remains uncertain.
